article_title: I finally found a way to firewall Android apps without a VPN tunnel
core_thesis: The article presents a method to block internet access for specific Android apps without using a VPN tunnel, which typically imposes limitations like inability to use a real VPN, battery drain, and persistent notifications. By combining two open-source apps—Shizuku (which grants elevated permissions via ADB) and ShizuWall (a firewall app that uses those permissions)—users can achieve app-level internet blocking natively, without a background VPN service.
detailed_summary: The article explains that Android lacks a built-in feature to block individual apps from accessing the internet. Existing third-party firewall apps rely on a VPN tunnel, which prevents simultaneous use of a real VPN, drains battery, and leaves a persistent notification. The author introduces a workaround using Shizuku and ShizuWall. Shizuku leverages Android's Wireless Debugging feature (part of Developer Options) to grant elevated system privileges without root. ShizuWall then uses those privileges to enforce firewall rules directly, without a VPN tunnel. The setup involves enabling Developer Options, turning on Wireless Debugging, pairing Shizuku with a 6-digit code, starting the Shizuku server, then opening ShizuWall, selecting Shizuku as the working mode, and configuring firewall rules. ShizuWall offers four modes: Default (block selected apps), Adaptive (block more without restarting), Whitelist (block all except allowed), and Smart Foreground (block all except the active app). Once configured, the firewall works without a background service. The article also provides a troubleshooting tip: if errors occur, users can switch to the 'LABD & Daemon' setup, which creates a lightweight background daemon (not a VPN). The article concludes that this method allows using a real VPN alongside the firewall.

entities.name: Shizuku
entities.role: Open-source app that grants elevated system permissions via ADB/Wireless Debugging, enabling firewall functionality without root.

entities.name: ShizuWall
entities.role: Open-source firewall app that uses Shizuku to block app internet access natively, without a VPN tunnel.

entities.name: Android Debug Bridge (ADB)
entities.role: Command-line tool used by Shizuku to obtain elevated privileges for app-level firewall control.

entities.name: Wireless Debugging
entities.role: Android Developer Option that allows ADB connections over Wi-Fi, used to pair Shizuku without a PC.

entities.name: Faisal Rasool
entities.role: Author of the article, a feature writer at How-To Geek with expertise in Android and privacy.

key_data_points: Android lacks a native feature to block individual apps from internet access, forcing users to rely on third-party solutions that typically require a VPN tunnel. (🟢 Relevant to your Red Teaming: understanding Android's limitations helps in assessing attack surface and device hardening.)

VPN-based firewalls prevent simultaneous use of a real VPN, drain battery, and create a persistent notification. (🟢 Relevant to your homelab/DevOps: if you manage Android devices, this method preserves VPN functionality for secure tunnels.)

Shizuku uses Android's Wireless Debugging (ADB over Wi-Fi) to grant elevated permissions without root, enabling system-level operations. (🟢 Relevant to your programming: ADB is a key tool for Android automation and debugging; understanding its privilege model is useful for scripting.)

ShizuWall offers four firewall modes: Default, Adaptive, Whitelist, and Smart Foreground, allowing flexible control over app internet access. (🟢 Relevant to your needs: whitelist mode is ideal for security-focused setups where only trusted apps should have network access.)

The firewall works without a constant background service; after configuration, Shizuku and ShizuWall can be closed. (🟢 Relevant to your homelab: efficient resource usage is critical for low-power devices like Android-based servers.)

If Shizuku fails, users can switch to 'LABD & Daemon' setup, which creates a lightweight background daemon (not a VPN) for stability. (🟢 Relevant to your troubleshooting: provides a fallback method for reliability.)
methodology_steps: Install Shizuku from the Play Store and ShizuWall from its GitHub releases (APK).
Enable Developer Options on Android: go to Settings > About Phone > tap Build Number 7 times.
In Developer Options, enable 'Wireless Debugging' toggle.
Open Shizuku app, tap 'Pairing' to show a notification with a pairing code field.
Go back to Developer Options > Wireless Debugging, tap 'Pair with 6-digit code' to generate a code.
Enter the generated code into the Shizuku notification to pair the device.
Once paired, tap 'Start' in Shizuku to start the Shizuku server.
Open ShizuWall, select 'Shizuku' as the working mode, and grant permission when prompted.
In ShizuWall settings (gear icon), toggle 'Skip enable confirmation' for convenience.
Choose a Firewall Mode: Default (block selected), Adaptive (block more without restart), Whitelist (block all except allowed), or Smart Foreground (block all except active app).
Select the apps to block from the list (e.g., keyboard apps, ad-heavy apps).
Tap the 'Firewall' toggle at the top to enable blocking. The firewall will work without a background service.
Optional: If errors occur, go to ShizuWall settings > 'LABD & Daemon' setup, pair with Wireless Debugging again, and connect to start a daemon.
conclusions: ShizuWall combined with Shizuku provides a native-like firewall for Android without the overhead of a VPN tunnel, allowing simultaneous VPN use and reducing battery drain. This is a significant improvement over traditional VPN-based firewalls.

The method is entirely open-source and free (if using the GitHub version of ShizuWall), making it accessible for privacy-conscious users and developers who want granular control over app network access.
The setup requires enabling Developer Options and Wireless Debugging, which may be a barrier for non-technical users but is straightforward for programmers and homelab enthusiasts.
The article confirms that until Google adds a native firewall, this is the closest alternative, and it works on non-rooted devices, preserving warranty and security.
personal_relevance_summary: This article is highly relevant to your interests in cybersecurity, programming, and homelabbing. As a Red Teamer, understanding Android's permission model and how to control app network access without root is valuable for both offensive (e.g., isolating malware samples) and defensive (e.g., hardening devices) scenarios. For your programming work, the use of ADB and Shizuku's API could inspire automation scripts or custom firewall solutions. In a homelab context, this method allows you to run Android devices as secure sensors or servers with precise network controls, preserving VPN functionality for other traffic. The open-source nature of both tools aligns with your preference for transparent, customizable software.
extra_advice: Consider scripting the Shizuku pairing process using ADB commands (e.g., `adb pair` and `adb connect`) to automate firewall setup across multiple Android devices in your homelab. You could also explore Shizuku's API to build custom network filtering tools for Red Teaming exercises, such as blocking C2 traffic from specific apps during a simulation.
